docs.title: Home

docs.text: Current status: JORN is in a founding and feasibility phase. It is not accepting formal submissions and has not published or certified any research. JORN in one sentence ¶ JORN helps researchers prepare, assess, review, publish, connect, and certify complete open research records. The problem JORN addresses ¶ Computational research rarely exists as one document. A manuscript may be in a preprint repository, data in a specialist archive, software in a source repository and preservation service, and protocols or environments elsewhere. A reader can struggle to determine which versions were actually reviewed and how they relate to the editorial decision. JORN is designed to add an accountable scholarly-publication layer without taking over the work of suitable repositories. JORN does not replace research repositories. It provides the editorial, publication, and stewardship layer that turns distributed research objects into one citable and accountable journal record. What makes JORN a journal ¶ JORN will qualify as a journal because it will receive eligible submissions, appoint qualified editors, coordinate independent peer review, request and evaluate revisions, make formal acceptance or rejection decisions, publish accepted work under the JORN title, register publication metadata, and maintain corrections, retractions, appeals, and continuing status. Technical checks or a certification seal alone do not constitute journal publication. What makes the record distinctive ¶ After acceptance, JORN will publish a permanent JORN Open Research Record containing the citation and DOI, abstract, exact accepted manuscript, data and software versions, computational environment, declarations, peer-review reports, author responses, editorial decision, certification profile, optional badge evidence, limitations, and current status. The JORN DOI will identify this journal record. Repository identifiers will continue to identify their underlying research objects. Structured metadata will connect them. What JORN certifies ¶ The universal JORN Certified Open Research Record seal will state that JORN peer-reviewed, accepted, published, and connected the exact versions identified in the Open Research Record. It applies only to the assessment scope described there. Certification does not guarantee permanent correctness, complete error detection, open access to all data, computational reproduction of every result, independent replication, or immunity from future software and platform changes. JORN is therefore different from a standalone reproducibility-checking service. Computational checking may contribute optional evidence, but acceptance requires formal scientific and editorial judgment. Institutional relationships ¶ Participant Role during the founding phase Open Research Network The broader collaboration and community initiative JORN The proposed journal and formal scholarly-publication program Open Science Labs A supporting organisation helping develop the initiative Legal publisher [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE LAUNCH] Editorial leadership Must hold independent authority over journal decisions; appointments are [TO BE CONFIRMED] Infrastructure providers May supply tools or services but will not control editorial outcomes Funders and partners May support operations but will receive no editorial privilege JORN is being designed as a community-governed journal. It should not be described as community-governed in operation until governance bodies, appointments, decision rights, terms, voting arrangements, and accountability mechanisms exist. Present status ¶ The title, legal publisher, platform, DOI and preservation arrangements, editorial appointments, pilot dates, final policies, partner relationships, funding route, and submission process require confirmation. The website labels proposed features explicitly so that a design intention is not mistaken for an operational service. Explore the publishing model Read the founding pilot plan Six connected functions ¶ The public Open Science Implementation Guide helps anyone assess and improve a research package. A Readiness Check and approved, human-supervised AI triage may identify missing evidence before review. Scientific review uses qualified external disciplinary communities where suitable and JORN-appointed reviewers where none is available. Open-science and artifact assessment evaluates exact data, software, workflows, identifiers, access and reproducibility claims. Evidence-based certification publishes a dimensional profile, verified claims, exceptions, badges and limitations. The JORN Open Research Record connects the accepted package, reviews and decision under one maintained JORN DOI. What JORN does not replace ¶ JORN does not seek to replace repositories, preprint servers, disciplinary peer-review communities, software-review organisations, data archives, protocol platforms, reproducibility services or research infrastructures. It defines interoperable outcomes, routes and coordinates review, recognises qualifying evidence, fills gaps, makes the final journal decision and maintains the authoritative record.

docs.text: Governance status: proposed. No body listed below should be treated as appointed or operational until its membership and mandate are published. Proposed decision structure ¶ Body or role Purpose and authority Membership and appointment Public outputs Legal publisher [unresolved] Holds legal and publishing responsibility, contracts, domain and DOI authority, privacy accountability, and closure obligations [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE LAUNCH] Publisher identity, legal notices, accountability route Steering Council [proposed] Mission, long-term strategy, financial oversight, senior appointments, major policy approval, succession Multiple institutions; transparent nomination and appointment; fixed renewable terms Minutes, decisions, annual and financial reports, interests Editor-in-Chief [proposed] Independent authority over editorial standards, appointments, escalated decisions, appeals separation, and board performance Open criteria and declared interests; appointed for a fixed term Editorial report, standards, recusal and delegation record Managing Editor [proposed] Intake, process, reviewer coordination, records, deadlines, author communication, and policy implementation Skilled paid or appropriately supported role; accountable to defined authority Workflow statistics and operational report Editorial Board [proposed] Scope, reviewer selection, manuscript decisions, criteria development, and section expertise Multiple institutions and relevant computational, methodological, software, and data expertise Names, affiliations, ORCIDs, terms, interests, decision policy Research Integrity Group [proposed] Independent advice on misconduct, expressions of concern, retractions, ethics, and complex conflicts Relevant expertise with strict recusal and confidentiality rules Procedures and anonymised or public outcomes where appropriate Community Advisory Group [proposed] Structured advice from researchers, libraries, infrastructure, independent scholars, and affected communities Transparent selection; advisory rather than manuscript-decision authority Recommendations and responses from decision-making bodies Technical and Metadata Group [proposed] Platform, identifiers, repository connections, preservation, accessibility, privacy, security, and portability Relevant specialists; conflicts with vendors declared Technical decisions, incident summaries, accessibility and preservation updates Detailed appointment methods, term lengths, renewal limits, voting rights, removal, resignation, quorum, conflict rules, and record publication are [TO BE CONFIRMED] . Editorial independence ¶ Editorial leaders must control individual manuscript decisions. Open Science Labs, Open Research Network participants, the legal publisher, funders, donors, partners, and infrastructure providers must not direct an acceptance, rejection, reviewer report, or certification outcome. Submissions involving an editor, council member, supporter, or close collaborator require independent assignment and documented recusal. Financial or institutional support cannot confer fast-track review or privileged access. Responsibilities that need named owners ¶ Before launch, JORN must assign responsibility for policy approval; editor appointments and performance; appeals and complaints; research-integrity investigations; privacy and security incidents; metadata and preservation; accessibility; financial approval and disclosure; annual reporting; succession; and orderly journal closure. Combining these responsibilities in one informal group would create unresolved conflicts and weak accountability. Appeals, complaints, and removal ¶ An appeal should be considered by an editor or panel not responsible for the original decision. Complaints about conduct or process need a confidential route and escalation beyond the person complained about. Governance documents should define investigation authority, evidence handling, recusal, timeframes, outcomes, and protection against retaliation. Appointments must have published routes for resignation, non-renewal, temporary suspension, and removal for misconduct, unmanaged conflict, sustained non-performance, or loss of community trust—subject to fair process. Reporting and consultation ¶ At minimum, an annual public report should cover submissions and decisions, turnaround and workload, reviewer and evaluator participation, appeals and complaints, corrections and retractions, certification changes, funding sources and expenditure categories, conflicts, accessibility, preservation, security incidents where disclosable, and planned policy changes. Material policy revisions should include a public consultation or a published explanation when consultation is not possible. Current unresolved decisions ¶ Legal publisher and contractual responsibility Ownership of the title, domain, DOI account, and journal assets Editor-in-Chief and Managing Editor appointments Board composition, appointment method, terms, and removal Decision rights and voting rules for every proposed body Appeals, complaints, integrity, and conflict procedures Funding authority, financial reporting, and editorial safeguards Privacy controller, security ownership, preservation, succession, and closure An editorial-board page should be published only after appointments are verified. It should list names, affiliations, ORCIDs, roles, terms, interests, and declarations rather than placeholder members. Decisions requiring explicit governance ¶ Named, unconflicted authorities must approve AI systems and model changes; monitor triage performance and incidents; decide triage appeals; recognise or withdraw external-review evidence; appoint internal reviewers; include or remove directory services; approve partnerships and data flows; approve, correct or withdraw badges; version criteria; and defend editorial independence. No AI provider, recommended service, review community, funder or partner may control individual publication decisions. docs.text: How the overlay works ¶ Authors place manuscripts and supporting objects in repositories appropriate to each object. JORN does not merely point to those deposits. It conducts editorial screening, appoints reviewers, assesses revisions, makes a formal decision, and publishes accepted work under the JORN title. Layer Holds or performs Continuing responsibility External repositories Manuscript files, data, software releases, protocols, preregistrations, and version history Object access and preservation under repository terms JORN editorial process Screening, editor assignment, scientific review, optional artifact or computational assessment, revision, and decision Fair process, conflicts, integrity, appeals, and decision quality JORN Open Research Record Citation, DOI, abstract, accepted versions, connections, reports, decision, certification profile, and current status Metadata, evidence, corrections, retractions, and continuity What JORN publishes ¶ The primary publication is the JORN Open Research Record . It identifies the exact accepted manuscript and every supporting object within the review scope; states which objects were unavailable, restricted, or not applicable; presents the editorial history; and records the scope and limits of certification. The manuscript may remain in its repository rather than being silently copied into an independent, unversioned journal file. JORN must nevertheless retain enough metadata and editorial evidence to preserve the meaning of its decision. The certificate supports the publication; it does not replace it ¶ The JORN Certified Open Research Record seal is a status attached after JORN has reviewed, accepted, and published the work. It links back to the Open Research Record. A technical certificate, successful software run, or badge without a formal editorial decision is not a JORN publication. Optional badges document specific additional assessments and do not create levels of publication quality. Identifiers have different purposes ¶ The JORN DOI identifies the journal Open Research Record. A manuscript or preprint identifier identifies the repository-hosted manuscript and its version history. Data identifiers identify exact datasets or access records. Archived-release identifiers identify preserved software releases. Protocol and preregistration identifiers identify their own research objects. Crossref- and DataCite-compatible relationships should express connections such as version, supplement, software, data, review, correction, and update. The DOI registration agency and metadata workflow are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE PUBLICATION] . Exact versions and computational environments ¶ Acceptance applies to an exact manuscript version. Software should have an archived release separate from the changing development repository. The record should identify source commit, release identifier, dependencies, container or environment specification where available, operating context, and assessment date. If authors revise work after acceptance, JORN should publish a correction, update, or superseding record according to the significance of the change. It must not silently move certification to a different object version. If an external object changes or disappears ¶ JORN should monitor critical links, retain deposited metadata and checksums where appropriate, use preservation services, and direct authors toward repositories with durable versioning. If an object becomes unavailable, the Open Research Record will state the change, its effect on access or assessment, and whether the certification remains current, corrected, suspended, superseded, or withdrawn. JORN remains responsible for its citation, DOI, editorial evidence, status history, and notices even when it does not host the underlying files. Architecture diagram ¶ The intended public diagram has three levels: Research repositories: exact manuscript, data, software release, source, environment, protocol, and preregistration. Journal process: screening, editor, scientific review, optional artifact evaluation, optional computational check, revision, and decision. JORN Open Research Record: citation and DOI, connected identifiers, public editorial history, certification profile, badges and limitations, and continuing status. Arrows should show version-specific metadata flowing into the journal process and the final record linking back to—not replacing—the repository objects. Platform and preservation direction ¶ JORN should evaluate a mature overlay platform before building custom submission software. Relevant benchmarks include Episciences and repository-based review models. Selection must consider privacy, role-based access, public-review support, metadata export, DOI workflows, preservation, accessibility, security, and migration. Platform selection, repository eligibility, preservation partners, backup schedule, and closure plan are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE PILOT LAUNCH] . JORN as an orchestration layer ¶ JORN is designed to connect repositories, open-science services, approved AI-assisted screening, disciplinary review communities, directly appointed JORN reviewers, technical assessors and the final citable Open Research Record. It does not take over their specialist preservation or evaluation functions. JORN prescribes outcomes and evidence, not vendors. The journal coordinates the process, assesses whether external evidence is sufficient, fills gaps, makes the publication decision and maintains the authoritative record. Recommendations do not imply partnership; recognised assessments and integrations require documented decisions in the relationship register.

Policy status model ¶ Every policy page will display: Status: draft, public consultation, approved, or operational Responsible body: author, reviewer, approver, and operational owner Version and effective date: including the rules applicable to each submission History: consultation, changes, prior versions, and reasons for revision Contact and escalation: questions, appeals, complaints, and urgent concerns Founding policy register ¶ Policy group Required coverage Current status Owner / version / date Scope and submissions Article types, eligibility, repository criteria, exact versions, screening, withdrawal Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Peer review and decisions Review model, identity, confidentiality, conflicts, decision taxonomy, editor submissions Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] JORN certification Universal claim, evidence, limitations, criteria version, correction, suspension, supersession, withdrawal Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Evidence badges Functional artifact and reproduced-result criteria, evaluator roles, partial outcomes, status changes Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] No aggregate score Prohibition on stars, grades, medals, percentages, composite trust or openness scores Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Data and materials Open, controlled, embargoed, synthetic, non-shareable, and inapplicable categories; access review Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Software and environments Source and archived release, tests, dependencies, environment, preservation, licensing Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Metadata and identifiers JORN DOI, research-object identifiers, relationship metadata, corrections to external objects Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Publication ethics Authorship, CRediT, plagiarism, fabrication, manipulation, interests, funding, ethics, consent Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Artificial intelligence Permitted uses, disclosure, authorship exclusion, confidentiality, reviewer and editor responsibilities Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Corrections and integrity Updates, corrections, concerns, retractions, investigations, evidence, notifications Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Appeals and complaints Grounds, independence, intake, timeframes, escalation, records, protection from retaliation Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Privacy and security Controller, lawful basis, retention, access, sensitive submissions, incidents, service providers Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Accessibility Target standard, testing, alternative formats, issue reporting, remediation, exceptions Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Rights, licenses, and fees Author rights, CC BY preference, object licenses, Diamond OA commitment, waivers if ever relevant Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Sponsorship and independence Funding disclosure, prohibited influence, advertising, donor and partner conflicts Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Preservation and closure Metadata export, editorial evidence, backups, succession, domain and DOI continuity, journal closure Planning [TO BE CONFIRMED] Current policy direction ¶ Diamond Open Access ¶ JORN is designed to charge neither authors nor readers. Authors should retain copyright, with CC BY 4.0 as the preferred article-content license. These commitments require confirmation by the legal publisher and a sustainable funding model. Open peer review ¶ The proposed default is to publish reports, author responses, and decisions after acceptance. Reviewers may sign by consent or remain confidential. Restricted disclosure is allowed where openness creates credible risk. Terms must be known before participation. Responsible data access ¶ JORN will not equate public data with responsible data. A justified controlled-access, embargoed, synthetic, or non-shareable arrangement can be eligible if the journal can evaluate it lawfully and securely. Certification and scoring ¶ Certification will be bounded to exact versions and the assessment described. JORN will not publish an aggregate score, trust percentage, grade, star rating, or publication level. Optional evidence badges remain separate from the universal Certified Open Research Record seal. Accessibility ¶ JORN intends to use WCAG 2.2 AA as a design and testing target. It should not claim conformance until a documented audit has been completed. The final policy must define testing, ownership, alternative formats, reporting, remediation times, and known exceptions. Before any policy becomes operational ¶ The legal publisher, responsible governance body, editor authority, privacy contact, complaint route, and public version repository must be confirmed. Approved policies should remain accessible after revision so readers can determine which version governed a particular Open Research Record. Expanded policy set required for the pilot ¶ The register must include separate, versioned policies for AI-assisted triage, AI confidentiality, AI disclosure and human reconsideration; external scientific review; internal reviewer recruitment; review-platform use; implementation levels; synthetic data; research software; service recommendations; external-assessment recognition; certification and badge approval or withdrawal; Open Research Record metadata and machine-readable exports; and appeals, complaints, corrections, expressions of concern and retractions. Ethical and editorial design audit ¶ Before any policy becomes operational, the approving body must confirm that JORN: does not equate AI triage with peer review or let AI make publication, certification, appointment, correction or retraction decisions; does not allow autonomous AI rejection during the pilot and provides factual correction and human reconsideration; excludes prestige, predicted impact and protected characteristics from screening; protects confidential, personal and restricted information and discloses approved AI inputs and retention; uses qualified external communities where appropriate and recruits JORN reviewers where none is suitable; keeps the final decision with an unconflicted JORN editor and distinguishes public comments from commissioned review; does not equate openness with validity, restriction with poor practice, or synthetic data with anonymity; uses dimensional levels rather than rankings and permits equivalent services that meet published outcomes; distinguishes recommendations, recognised providers, integrations and formal partners; and limits every certificate and badge to the exact version, evidence, criteria, assessor, date and stated limitations.

docs.title: Ethical and editorial design audit

docs.text: Status: proposed pilot framework. JORN has not approved an AI system, provider, secure environment, performance threshold, or operational triage policy. Why use assisted screening ¶ A research package can span many repositories, identifiers and versions. An approved system may help editors find missing objects, inconsistent links and documentation gaps, then draft a remediation and expertise-routing report. It must expose its evidence and uncertainty rather than predict impact or acceptance. AI checks whether the research package appears ready for responsible human evaluation. An external field community reviews it when available; otherwise, JORN recruits qualified reviewers. A JORN editor remains accountable for the final decision. Three screening outcomes ¶ Ready for human review ¶ The public or authorised package appears sufficiently complete for an editor to consider review routing. An editor must confirm this outcome. It does not mean the research is correct, ethical, publishable, certified, or likely to be accepted. Author action required ¶ Specific missing, inaccessible, inconsistent, or insufficiently documented elements should be addressed. During the pilot, this is a request for remediation—not an autonomous rejection. Authors can correct facts, explain an exception, or request human screening. Human editorial triage required ¶ Uncertainty, sensitive content, unusual methods, restricted access, ethics or governance questions, conflicting evidence, or poor model coverage requires direct assessment. Sensitive flags are not published automatically. Permitted checks ¶ AI may assist with scope classification; completeness; links among objects; DOI and identifier extraction; licence and version detection; data, software and synthetic-data statements; internal consistency; claim–evidence mapping; reporting-guideline prompts; reviewer-expertise profiles; external-community suggestions; possible conflicts for human investigation; and a remediation report. Deterministic tools are preferred for DOI resolution, checksum comparison, file validation, link checking, licence detection and metadata-schema validation. Generative models should be limited to interpretation, explanation, classification and draft synthesis. Prohibited judgments ¶ AI must not autonomously decide correctness, importance, novelty, misconduct, ethical adequacy, medical-model safety, reviewer appointment, acceptance, certification, corrections, expressions of concern or retraction. Screening must not use protected characteristics, prestige, institutional reputation, geography, citation counts or predicted impact as proxies for merit. Human oversight and author rights ¶ During the pilot, a human editor confirms every report and every recommendation to proceed. Human–AI disagreement is recorded. Authors may inspect the report, correct factual errors, explain missing elements, request human reconsideration, and use an appeal route separate from the original operator [TO BE CONFIRMED] . Formal reviewer appointment always remains human-controlled. AI-Assisted Initial Screening Report ¶ The private report should record system and model version, date, criteria version, exact manuscript and artifact versions, materials supplied, scope, completeness, gaps, ethical or governance flags, uncertainty, suggested expertise and communities, routing outcome, human confirmation, author corrections, and audit history. Confirmed factual elements may later be disclosed in the Open Research Record. Unverified integrity, privacy or ethics concerns must not be published automatically. Confidentiality and security ¶ Public preprints and artifacts may be analysed under a disclosed process. Do not upload unpublished manuscripts, confidential reviews, reviewer identities, private correspondence, controlled patient data, personal data, ethics documentation, security-sensitive code or proprietary material to consumer or unapproved AI systems. Sensitive processing requires an approved secure environment, no provider training on submitted content, retention and deletion rules, access control, logging, encryption, lawful processing, contracts, and privacy and security review. Pilot evaluation ¶ JORN should publish aggregate performance by discipline and package type: false-ready and false-action rates, missed items, unsupported flags, human overrides, author corrections, accessibility, cost and time. Launch thresholds, audit method, incident route and model-change controls are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE OPERATION] . Read the full editorial process Review the governance framework

docs.text: Status: proposed pilot activity. JORN does not promise artifact assessment, computational reproduction or secure-data verification for every work. Possible assessments ¶ Documentation and availability sufficient for the declared purpose Installation, tests or core functions under a named environment Usability of a specified dataset or synthetic companion package Execution of a bounded workflow and comparison of named outputs Synthetic-data utility or privacy under specialist criteria Authorised verification against controlled real data Required report ¶ Every finding identifies object and version, evaluator and conflicts, date, criteria, environment, commands or procedures, evidence, tolerances, successful outcomes, differences, failures, excluded components and limitations. Partial or unsuccessful checks remain visible and informative. Critical distinctions ¶ Functional software is not the same as reproduced research results. Reproduction of results is not independent replication. A synthetic workflow run is not secure real-data verification. A technical evaluator does not make the journal’s scientific decision. Qualifying evidence may support an evidence badge , but badge approval requires human review and publication acceptance. Read the badge definitions Read software guidance

docs.text: Formal submissions are not open. This guidance supports pilot planning and may change before any operational intake. Before proposing work ¶ Use the Implementation Guide and Readiness Check . Deposit and version the manuscript and applicable research objects; prepare identifiers and licences or access terms; document restrictions, missing elements and next actions; and identify discipline-specific expertise. Package checklist ¶ Provide authors, ORCIDs, affiliations, RORs and CRediT roles; exact manuscript version; data and synthetic-data statements; source and archived software release; environment and workflow; figure provenance; protocol and preregistration; ethics, consent and governance; funding and interests; accessibility; preservation; external reviews; suggested review communities; and known limitations. What happens next ¶ Deterministic checks and approved AI-assisted screening may identify remediation needs. An editor confirms triage, assesses scope and ethics, chooses a review route, and remains responsible for reviewer appointment and the final decision. Passing screening is not acceptance. Consent and confidentiality ¶ A future form must separately explain AI use, inputs, retention, providers, human review, correction rights and alternatives. Do not submit confidential or restricted material until JORN publishes an approved privacy notice and secure route. Propose pilot work See the complete editorial process

docs.text: Status: proposed pilot definitions. No JORN badge has been issued. Criteria, evaluators and approval authority are **[TO BE CONFIRMED]**. Artifacts Evaluated—Functional ¶ An independent evaluator confirmed that the specified artifacts were sufficiently documented, available to the evaluator, and functional for the declared purpose. The report names versions, environment, procedures, outcomes, differences and exclusions. It does not establish scientific validity or general reusability. Computational Results Reproduced ¶ An independent evaluator executed the specified workflow and obtained the identified outputs within stated comparison criteria or tolerance. The claim is limited to those outputs, objects and environment. It is not independent replication. External Software Review Recognised ¶ The identified software version underwent a qualifying external review whose scope, process and evidence JORN evaluated and recognised. It does not imply a partnership, validate the paper, or extend to later software versions. Possible future claims ¶ Only after pilot evidence and policy approval, JORN may consider Artifacts Evaluated—Reusable , Preregistration Assessed , Synthetic Data Utility Assessed , Synthetic Data Privacy Assessed , Secure Real-Data Verification , Independent Replication , or Independent Clinical Validation . These claims are distinct and must never be presented as equivalent. Required badge record ¶ Every badge names exact object, version, assessor, conflicts, date, criteria version, evidence, checks, successful outcomes, differences, exclusions, limitations and current status. Readers can open the underlying report. A self-assessment or AI output cannot generate an official badge. Partial, unsuccessful and changed outcomes ¶ Reports should disclose informative partial or unsuccessful checks even when no badge is awarded. Material object, criteria or evidence changes trigger reassessment. JORN may correct, suspend, supersede or withdraw a badge while preserving its history. Read artifact assessment Understand certification

docs.text: Status: draft founding framework. JORN has not certified or published any research. Criteria, approvers, DOI route and withdrawal policy remain unconfirmed. Three distinct concepts ¶ JORN Open Research Record: permanent journal publication produced after formal editorial acceptance. JORN Certified Open Research Record: status showing that JORN reviewed, accepted, published and connected the exact objects and evidence described in the record. Underlying research objects: repository- or platform-held manuscript, data, software, models, figures, workflows, protocols, registrations and reviews. A technical check, readiness report, AI screen, external review, badge or certificate alone is not journal publication. Proposed certification statement ¶ This research was peer-reviewed, accepted, and published by JORN. The certification profile records how the exact manuscript, research objects, reviews, and evidence identified in this Open Research Record satisfied the applicable JORN criteria. The statement always links to the permanent record and its current status. Implementation levels and verification states ¶ Each applicable dimension receives Level 0 Not documented , Level 1 Transparent , Level 2 Accessible, citable, and responsibly managed , or Level 3 Independently verified . The evidence state—self-assessed, evidence supplied, AI-assisted screening, or JORN verified—must appear separately. Only JORN verified evidence may support official certification. Controlled access can satisfy Level 2 when the restriction, metadata, governance and responsible verification route meet the criteria. No level is a quality, impact or prestige score. Dimension Level Verification state Exact evidence Differences and limits Manuscript 0–3 One of four states Identifier and version Unavailable or excluded elements Data and synthetic data 0–3 One of four states Access, governance, provenance and version Public and controlled evidence distinguished Software and environment 0–3 One of four states Release, source, tests and environment Scope of function or review Review 0–3 JORN verified for publication Reports, responses and decision Route, expertise and disclosure limits If an overall implementation designation is displayed, it is the minimum across applicable mandatory dimensions. This designation describes implementation of the JORN Open Science Framework. It is not a measure of scientific quality, importance, correctness, novelty, or impact. Every verified claim records ¶ Exact object and version; assessor; date; criteria version; evidence; checks; successful outcomes; differences; exclusions; limitations; and current status. AI may organise evidence but cannot issue a certificate or badge. Bounded evidence badges ¶ Pilot badges, if approved, are independent findings: Artifacts Evaluated—Functional , Computational Results Reproduced , and External Software Review Recognised . A record may have any combination or none. Badges do not create tiers of journal publication. What certification does not establish ¶ Certification does not guarantee permanent correctness, complete error detection, public access to every object, ethical or regulatory approval, safety, computational reproduction beyond named outputs, independent replication, future technical execution, novelty, importance or impact. Status and correction ¶ Certification and badges may be current, corrected, superseded, suspended, withdrawn or associated with a retracted publication. JORN preserves the original history and publishes reasons to the greatest ethical and lawful extent. Review implementation levels Read evidence badges

docs.text: Status: draft guidance. JORN has not confirmed a secure-data capability. Do not send restricted or personal data until an approved route and privacy notice are published. Accepted access categories ¶ JORN’s proposed framework recognises open real data, controlled-access real data, secure-environment access, embargoed data, synthetic companion or evaluation data, simulation-generated data, public metadata with restricted records, data not publicly shareable with a reviewed justification, and not applicable. Restrictions do not automatically reduce the implementation level. The record must describe why access is limited, the governing authority, public metadata, request or verification route, eligible users, conditions, version, retention or embargo, and what JORN could and could not inspect. Minimum evidence ¶ Persistent identifier and exact version, or a documented reason one cannot be issued Data dictionary, provenance, collection or generation methods, and quality limitations Licence for open data or clear terms and governance for restricted data Ethics, consent, community or Indigenous governance and lawful-access documentation where applicable Relationship to manuscript, software, figures, protocols and derived objects Responsible preservation and change-control route Citation instructions Responsible verification ¶ A public metadata record plus a synthetic companion dataset can support software testing but does not establish that the reported analysis matches restricted real data. A separate secure verification may be required. The Open Research Record must distinguish materials accessible to readers from materials available only to an authorised evaluator. What JORN will not claim ¶ Data availability does not prove validity. Restriction does not prove weak practice. A synthetic dataset is not automatically anonymous. An evaluator’s access does not entitle JORN to redistribute the data. Every verified claim must stay within the exact evidence and access scope. Read the synthetic-data guidance Use the Readiness Check

docs.text: Workflow under development. Formal submissions are not open. Roles, service levels, forms, systems, criteria and policies require approval before operation. The 21 stages ¶ 01 Use the Implementation Guide Authors assess applicable research objects, practices and evidence. 02 Complete readiness self-assessment Gaps, restrictions, external assessments and human-verification needs are recorded without a score. 03 Deposit and version objects The manuscript, data, software, workflows and other applicable materials receive exact versions, identifiers and access terms. 04 Receive proposal or submission During the founding phase, JORN receives only pilot expressions of interest—not formal journal submissions. 05 Run deterministic technical checks Approved tools resolve identifiers, validate files and links, detect metadata or licence fields, and compare checksums where appropriate. 06 Conduct AI-assisted triage If approved and consented, an AI system analyses authorised materials for readiness and routing—not acceptance. 07 Editor confirms triage A human confirms, changes or escalates every pilot result and records disagreement. 08 Screen scope, ethics and completeness An editor determines whether responsible evaluation is possible and requests remediation where appropriate. 09 Select scientific-review route The editor assesses external, coordinated or JORN internal review against the required expertise profile. 10 Use an external community where suitable Qualified, version-specific external review may reduce duplication under published recognition criteria. 11 Recruit JORN reviewers where needed When no suitable community is available, JORN appoints qualified scientific reviewers directly. 12 Fill expertise gaps Supplementary reviewers address gaps in external review, methods, statistics, governance or domain coverage. 13 Review open-science implementation Qualified reviewers verify documentation, identifiers, access, licences, versions, provenance and connections. 14 Assess artifacts where applicable Evaluators inspect function, usability, workflows, synthetic data, secure verification or reproduction for bounded claims. 15 Authors revise exact versions Responses and new repository versions preserve what changed and why. 16 Evaluate revisions Reviewers or editors check responses and changed objects within their expertise and declared scope. 17 JORN editor decides An unconflicted editor integrates all evidence and formally accepts or rejects the exact package. AI and badges do not decide. 18 Produce the Open Research Record JORN prepares the citation, DOI metadata, object relationships, reports, decisions, statements and status. 19 Approve certification and badges Human approvers verify each level and bounded claim against exact evidence and criteria. 20 Publish and preserve Accessible HTML, PDF, citation exports and structured metadata are released under the JORN title. 21 Maintain stewardship JORN manages corrections, updates, expressions of concern, withdrawal, retraction, links and certification status. Screening outcomes are not decisions ¶ “Ready for human review,” “author action required,” and “human editorial triage required” are routing outcomes. During the pilot, AI cannot autonomously reject work. Authors may correct factual errors and request human reconsideration. Decision taxonomy ¶ Decision Accountable actor Meaning Decline before review JORN editor Outside scope or not responsibly reviewable after human screening Major or minor revision JORN editor Specified changes are required Accept JORN editor Exact identified version accepted for JORN publication Reject JORN editor Publication criteria not met after accountable evaluation Withdraw Author or journal under policy Process ended with reason and record handling defined Pause JORN editor Ethics, integrity, access, expertise or legal question must be resolved Public review record ¶ The proposed default after acceptance is public commissioned reports, author responses and editorial decisions. Reviewers sign only by consent. External reports show their source and recognised scope. Public comments remain labelled as community feedback. Rejected or withdrawn work requires separate consent and policy. Appeals and integrity ¶ Appeals must go to an unconflicted editor or panel not responsible for the original decision. Complaints and integrity concerns require confidential intake, recusal, appropriate expertise, evidence handling and protection against retaliation. Routes and service targets are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE PILOT OPERATION] . Compare the review routes Read the Open Research Record specification

docs.text: Status: framework only. No assessment provider is currently recognised. Examples elsewhere on this site are candidates for investigation, not confirmed relationships. Recognition criteria ¶ JORN checks the provider’s governance and independence; assessor qualifications and appointment; conflicts; review criteria; exact object and version; evidence and report; author response; revision history; confidentiality and transfer permission; transparency; complaints and corrections; and continuing availability. Recognition decision ¶ A JORN editor or delegated qualified evaluator records whether evidence is recognised in full, in part, requires supplementary assessment, or is declined. The decision identifies the accepted claim and exclusions. Provider reputation alone is insufficient. Continuing controls ¶ Recognition is versioned, periodically reviewed and withdrawable. Material changes to criteria, ownership, funding, data handling, security or assessor selection trigger reassessment. Paid inclusion or preferential editorial treatment is prohibited. docs.text: Can I use the Guide without submitting? ¶ Yes. The draft JORN Open Science Implementation Guide is intended for researchers, groups, reviewers, librarians, stewards, institutions, students and independent researchers. Use does not imply endorsement, review, certification or acceptance. Are implementation levels quality scores? ¶ No. Levels describe documentation, responsible accessibility and independent verification for each applicable dimension. They do not score correctness, novelty, importance, ethics, safety, prestige or impact. JORN will not issue an aggregate openness or trust score. Does AI decide whether my paper is accepted? ¶ No. AI may assist with preliminary completeness and routing checks. An unconflicted JORN editor makes every formal accept or reject decision. What does passing AI triage mean? ¶ Only that the authorised package appears ready for responsible human evaluation under the applicable screening criteria. It does not mean the work is correct, ethical, certified, publishable or likely to be accepted. Can AI reject my work? ¶ Not autonomously during the pilot. “Author action required” is a remediation outcome. A human editor confirms triage and any editorial decline. Can I request human screening? ¶ The proposed policy provides factual correction and human reconsideration. The operator, time target and appeal route are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE OPERATION] . What information is supplied to AI? ¶ Normally only public preprints, public artifacts and authorised metadata under a disclosed process. Every report should identify the exact inputs. Will confidential data be uploaded to AI systems? ¶ Not to consumer or unapproved systems. Unpublished manuscripts, confidential reviews, reviewer identities, correspondence, controlled patient data, personal data, ethics files, security-sensitive code and proprietary material require an approved secure environment or must remain outside AI processing. Does JORN use external review communities? ¶ That is the proposed preferred route when an appropriate, qualified community is available. No relationship is currently confirmed. What happens when no community covers my field? ¶ A JORN handling editor defines the expertise profile and directly recruits qualified reviewers. Supplementary reviewers fill gaps in an external review when necessary. Who chooses JORN reviewers? ¶ An unconflicted JORN editor. AI may suggest expertise or communities but cannot appoint reviewers. Who makes the final decision? ¶ An accountable, unconflicted JORN editor after considering the manuscript, reviews, revisions, ethics, implementation evidence and technical reports. Does external review guarantee JORN acceptance? ¶ No. JORN may recognise all, part or none of an external review and may request supplementary review. JORN retains the publication decision. Can public comments count as peer review? ¶ They may inform the process but are not automatically formal peer review. Commissioned JORN review and recognised external review require qualified appointment, defined criteria, conflicts and a version-specific report. Can restricted or synthetic data be certified? ¶ Potentially. Restrictions can be responsible when justified, documented and reviewable. Synthetic data require purpose-specific utility and disclosure-risk evidence. A synthetic workflow run is not the same as checking results against restricted real data. Is synthetic data anonymous? ¶ Not automatically. The record must describe the disclosure-risk assessment, threat model, method, results and limits rather than infer anonymity from the word “synthetic.” Is JORN a journal or a certification service? ¶ A proposed scholarly overlay journal. It will conduct or recognise qualified review, oversee revision, make formal decisions, publish accepted work under the JORN title and maintain the record. Certification describes evidence attached to an accepted publication. What should readers cite? ¶ Cite the JORN Open Research Record for the accepted journal publication. Cite underlying data, software, protocols or other objects directly when using them. The JORN DOI and object identifiers serve different purposes. Is JORN accepting submissions or publishing articles? ¶ No. JORN has no accepted or certified publications and is not accepting formal submissions. Researchers may propose work for a future founding pilot, which does not guarantee review or publication. Who legally publishes JORN? ¶ [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE LAUNCH] . The legal publisher, ISSN, DOI registration, preservation, privacy controller, platform, editorial appointments and partnerships remain unconfirmed. Start with the Guide Review the founding status

docs.text: Status: draft guidance. JORN does not require a separate DOI for every decorative image, icon, chart panel, or minor visual element. Minimum requirements ¶ For applicable substantive objects, supply source data; generation code or a documented process; captions; licence and attribution; alternative text and accessibility information; provenance; version; and relationships to manuscript, data and software. A single versioned figure-and-source-material package is often clearer than assigning an identifier to every panel. Encourage a separate DOI or persistent identifier when a visual object is independently reusable or citable, is a primary research output, or forms a substantial microscopy, mapping, audiovisual or visualisation collection. Review questions ¶ Can a reader locate the source data and exact generation process? Are manipulations, exclusions and composite images disclosed? Do captions and alternative text convey the intended information? Are third-party rights and attributions clear? Is the version used in the accepted manuscript preserved? Does the accessible alternative serve an equivalent purpose? Traceability does not prove that the interpretation is correct; disciplinary reviewers remain responsible for the scientific use of the visual evidence. Use the Implementation Guide Review artifact assessment

docs.text: Status: draft guide for public testing. Using this guide does not imply JORN endorsement, peer review, certification, scientific validity, journal acceptance, or compliance with every legal or ethical obligation. Who the guide is for ¶ Researchers may use the guide while planning a project or preparing a paper. It is also designed for research groups, peer reviewers, librarians, research-software engineers, data stewards, funders, institutions, students, and independent researchers. Use does not require an intention to publish with JORN. Seven questions to answer ¶ How open and connected is the research now? What is missing or insufficiently documented? Why does each element matter? What practical action would improve it? Which standards, repositories, services, or communities could help? What evidence would JORN require for verification? Which claims require human, disciplinary, ethical, privacy, or technical review? Work through the dimensions ¶ Area Check Evidence to retain People and organisations Authors, ORCIDs, affiliations, ROR identifiers, CRediT roles, contacts Identifier records and contribution statement Research plan Protocol, preregistration, governance, deviations, ethics and consent Persistent records, approvals, amendments and explanations Manuscript Discoverable deposit, exact version, licence, methods and declarations Identifier, version, licence and checksum where appropriate Data Access category, identifier, version, documentation, licence or terms, provenance Repository record, data-management and access documentation Software and models Source, archived release, identifier, licence, tests, dependencies and citation Release DOI or SWHID, commit, environment and test evidence Workflows and environments Executable steps, notebooks, containers, dependency locks and expected outputs Versioned workflow and execution instructions Figures and media Source data, generation process, captions, attribution, alt text and provenance Versioned source-material package and relationships Review and evaluation Scientific review, open-science review and artifact checks kept distinct Reports, exact assessed versions, criteria and responses Stewardship Preservation, maintenance, corrections, access changes and status history Preservation route, owner and update plan The complete dimensional set also covers synthetic or simulated data, models, materials, licences, persistent identifiers, citation, accessibility, community or Indigenous governance, reproduction, replication, and maintenance. For each dimension ¶ Record its applicability, current implementation level , readiness state, evidence, gaps, next action, responsible person, dependencies, restrictions, and review needs. Do not calculate a numerical openness score. Assessment states ¶ Self-assessed: completed by an author or group without independent verification. Evidence supplied: links and documentation have been supplied, but JORN has not verified them. AI-assisted screening: an approved system generated a preliminary report on a public or securely authorised package. This is neither certification nor peer review. JORN verified: a qualified JORN editor, reviewer, evaluator, or recognised external assessor examined the evidence under a published criteria version. Only JORN verified evidence may support official JORN certification. Improve responsibly ¶ Open does not mean public at any cost. Research data should be as open as ethically, legally, and methodologically possible, and as restricted as necessary. Controlled access can meet the framework when the restriction, public metadata, governance, and responsible verification route are adequate. JORN prescribes outcomes and evidence, not vendors. The Services and Tools Guide lists selection criteria and examples; equivalent services are acceptable when they meet the same requirements. Test the draft guide ¶ Start with the readiness states, record your evidence, and note where the guidance is unclear. A public feedback route is [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE PUBLICATION] . Use the Readiness Check Review implementation levels

docs.text: Status: draft criteria. Levels are proposed for the founding pilot. They are not grades, prestige measures, or claims about scientific correctness. The four levels ¶ Level Name Minimum meaning Evidence 0 Not documented The object or practice is missing, unclear, or cannot be assessed. Gap recorded; reason where known 1 Transparent Existence, location, access status, owner, restrictions, limitations, and non-availability are disclosed. Versioned statement and links 2 Accessible, citable, and responsibly managed The object is appropriately deposited or registered, persistently identified, versioned, documented, connected, and governed by a licence or clear access terms. Exact object, metadata, identifier, version, access and preservation evidence 3 Independently verified JORN or a recognised external evaluator examined the exact object using published criteria. Assessor, date, criteria, report, results, differences, exclusions and limits Controlled or restricted materials may reach Level 2 when the restriction is justified and a responsible access or verification route exists. Level 3 is not automatically “better science”; it records an independent assessment of the specified implementation evidence. Dimensional profile ¶ The profile assesses independently: manuscript; identities and contributor roles; affiliations; protocol and preregistration; data and synthetic data; software and models; environment, workflows and notebooks; figures and media; methods and materials; licences; identifiers, versioning, citation and provenance; ethics, consent and governance; accessibility; scientific review; open-science review; artifact evaluation; reproduction; replication; maintenance and preservation. Dimension Applicable? Level Verification state Exact evidence Limitations Data Yes / no 0–3 Self / supplied / AI-screened / JORN verified Identifier and version Access or scope limits Software Yes / no 0–3 As above Release, source and environment Platform or test limits Scientific review Yes 0–3 JORN verified before publication Reports, response and decision Expertise and disclosure limits If JORN later displays an overall implementation designation, it will be the minimum level across applicable mandatory dimensions—not an average. This designation describes implementation of the JORN Open Science Framework. It is not a measure of scientific quality, importance, correctness, novelty, or impact. Measures JORN will not issue ¶ JORN will not publish numerical openness scores, percentages of trust, stars, A–F grades, rankings, prestige or predicted-impact measures, or one combined measure of scientific quality and openness. Read the certification framework Start with the Guide

docs.text: Network status: forming. Roles, selection, workload, recognition, payment, conflicts, and contact routes remain to be confirmed. No organisation is a formal partner unless JORN publishes that relationship. Institutional roles ¶ Open Research Network is the broader collaboration and community initiative. JORN is the proposed journal and formal scholarly-publication program. Open Science Labs supports development of the initiative. Editorial leadership must hold independent authority over journal decisions. Funders, partners, and infrastructure providers may support operations but receive no editorial privilege. The legal publisher is [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE LAUNCH] . Ways to participate ¶ 01 Potential pilot authors Propose eligible computational work and help test whether the author process is clear and proportionate. Pilot eligibility → 02 Scientific reviewers Evaluate contribution, methods, analysis, reporting, integrity, traceability, and limitations. Review process → 03 Artifact evaluators Assess specified software, data, documentation, and environments against bounded functional criteria. Badge definitions → 04 Computational codecheckers Execute specified workflows and compare identified outputs against transparent criteria or tolerance. Assessment evidence → 05 Editors Take responsibility for fair process, reviewer selection, conflicts, revisions, and formal decisions. Proposed authority → 06 Integrity advisers Help design and operate ethical review, allegations, expressions of concern, corrections, and retractions. Policy register → 07 Metadata & preservation specialists Develop identifier relationships, exports, repository monitoring, archiving, succession, and closure plans. Overlay architecture → 08 Accessibility & privacy advisers Review inclusive interfaces, alternative formats, data protection, secure access, retention, and incident response. Accessibility direction → 09 Institutions & libraries Explore durable staff, financial, governance, preservation, and consortium support without editorial privilege. Operating needs → 10 Infrastructure projects Advise on open interfaces, repository notifications, metadata, platform integration, portability, and preservation. Technical model → Selection and conflicts ¶ Editorial and assessment roles should be filled according to documented expertise, independence, conduct expectations, availability, and conflicts. Community membership, financial support, or institutional status must not guarantee appointment. For each assignment, JORN should consider recent collaboration, supervisory relationships, institutional dependence, financial interests, competition, advocacy roles, and other circumstances that could affect—or reasonably appear to affect—judgment. Workload, recognition, and payment ¶ Before recruitment, each role should have a realistic task description, estimated time, training and support, confidentiality terms, recognition route, and a clear statement of whether work is voluntary, paid, or institutionally supported. JORN should not build its sustainability model around unlimited unpaid labour. Managing-editor work, intensive artifact and computational evaluation, integrity cases, accessibility, privacy, metadata, and coordination require funded capacity. Standards and initiatives used as benchmarks ¶ JORN can learn from Episciences; Peer Community In and Peer Community Journal; ACM Artifact Review and Badging; CODECHECK; Center for Open Science badges and TOP Guidelines; COPE; DOAJ; DataCite; Crossref; COAR Notify; ORCID; CRediT; and Software Heritage. These are reference points, not claimed partners . Any future partnership will be named only after agreement and disclosure of its purpose and safeguards. Participation route ¶ The role application, privacy notice, selection process, expected workload, payment policy, training, conduct rules, and contact address are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE RECRUITMENT] . Until then, do not send confidential manuscripts, personal records, or sensitive declarations. Read the founding pilot plan Review and infrastructure ecosystem ¶ The proposed network includes external field-review communities; handling editors; JORN-commissioned scientific reviewers; open-science reviewers; artifact evaluators and codecheckers; synthetic-data, privacy and clinical specialists; repositories and libraries; software-review organisations; and metadata, accessibility and preservation experts. Roles remain distinct, appointed authority is explicit, and participation does not imply partnership. Suggested communities and services will be evaluated under published criteria. Until a relationship appears in the public register, it is only a recommendation or candidate for investigation.

docs.text: STATUS · BEING DESIGNED Formal journal submissions are not open ¶ JORN has not yet launched a publication platform or accepted a journal article. Expressions of interest help assess feasibility; they are not submissions and do not guarantee selection, review, certification, or publication. Propose work for consideration Pilot purpose ¶ The pilot should determine whether JORN can operate a credible and sustainable overlay-journal workflow for reproducible computational research. It will test the complete sequence from repository deposit and editorial screening through peer review, optional technical assessment, decision, metadata, publication, and continuing stewardship. Proposed pilot scope ¶ Only three submission types are proposed: Reproducible computational research articles in which code, data, environments, and computational workflows are central to the findings. Research software and computational methods papers with a clear scholarly contribution, documentation, tests, an archived release, and an identifiable reuse case. Reproducibility and replication reports describing independent attempts to reproduce computational results or replicate earlier findings, including informative unsuccessful attempts. What the pilot should evaluate ¶ Ability to recruit qualified, unconflicted editors and reviewers Time and workload for managing each editorial stage Feasibility of artifact and computational evaluation Clarity of author, reviewer, and evaluator guidance Reliability of version and research-object identification Metadata, DOI, repository, and preservation workflows Accessibility, privacy, security, and controlled-access handling Usefulness and limits of the certification profile and badges Complaints, appeals, corrections, and integrity procedures Real operating costs and sustainable staffing requirements Selection principles ¶ A technically sound expression of interest may not enter the pilot if the journal lacks appropriate editorial expertise, evaluator capacity, a suitable protected-access route, or a confirmed policy for the work involved. Pilot selection must not be presented as acceptance. Selected work will still undergo independent review and may be declined or rejected. Governance and timeline ¶ The pilot should begin only after the legal publishing entity, editorial authority, core policies, privacy responsibilities, platform, preservation route, and conflict procedures are confirmed. The number of records, dates, participant support, evaluation metrics, and reporting schedule remain [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE PILOT LAUNCH] . Results should be reported publicly, including workload, costs, completion and withdrawal rates, participant feedback, technical limitations, and changes required before public launch. From pilot to public launch ¶ A pilot does not commit JORN to launch. The responsible governance body should review the evidence and decide whether to proceed, revise the model, run another limited test, or stop. Formal submissions should open only when editorial capacity and required safeguards are operational. Expanded evaluation programme ¶ The founding pilot should also measure Guide usability; readiness-check usefulness; AI false positives, false negatives and unsupported flags; human overrides and author corrections; external-community routing and recognition; JORN reviewer recruitment; synthetic and controlled-data handling; service-directory usefulness; dimensional-profile consistency; and production of accessible, machine-readable Open Research Records. A staged implementation should separate publish now (clearly labelled guide and framework), pilot preparation (governance, policies, systems, people and measures), pilot operation (limited consented cases and public aggregate evaluation), public launch (only after safeguards and capacity are verified), and later expansion (new disciplines, badges or integrations supported by evidence).

docs.text: Status: specification for pilot testing. No operational checker or approved AI system is currently available. A completed check is not a submission, review, certification, or endorsement. What the check records ¶ For every applicable dimension, select one readiness state: Complete Partially complete Missing Not applicable Restricted with justification External assessment available Human verification required JORN verification complete Every selection should link to evidence and name the person responsible for the answer. “Not applicable” and restrictions require a short explanation; they are not penalties. What users receive ¶ For missing or partial items, the check should explain why the item matters, minimum requirements, next actions, acceptable evidence, service-selection criteria, equivalent alternatives, ethical or legal cautions, and discipline-specific guidance. An export should include criteria version, date, object versions, answers, evidence links, unresolved items, and an audit history. Self-assessment or AI-assisted assessment—not reviewed or certified by JORN That statement must appear prominently on every non-verified export. Official badges must never be generated from self-assessment alone. Self-assessment and AI assistance ¶ The self-assessment is controlled by the user. A future approved AI component may extract identifiers, detect licences and versions, find internal inconsistencies, and draft a remediation report. It must show its uncertainty and evidence. Authors can correct factual errors and request human reconsideration. An AI result remains AI-assisted screening , even when all items appear complete. It cannot establish scientific quality, ethical adequacy, misconduct, certification, or acceptance. See the AI-assisted triage framework . Example output ¶ Dimension Readiness Evidence Next action Verification need Manuscript Complete Preprint identifier and version Freeze submitted version Deterministic identifier check Data Restricted with justification Public metadata and access procedure Confirm lawful evaluator route Privacy and domain specialist Software Partially complete Source repository Archive a release and add licence Software evaluator Figures Missing None supplied Deposit source-data package and alt text Open-science review Scientific review External assessment available Version-specific reports Assess recognition criteria Handling editor This table is not an aggregate score. A complete package can still require substantial scientific revision, and an incomplete package may be improved before review. Privacy boundary ¶ Public objects may be checked under a disclosed process. Unpublished manuscripts, confidential reviews, reviewer identities, controlled patient data, personal data, ethics files, security-sensitive code, and proprietary material must not enter consumer or unapproved AI systems. Sensitive processing requires an approved secure environment and published controls. Use the Implementation Guide Read the triage safeguards

docs.text: No formal review-community relationships are confirmed. PCI, Review Commons, PREreview, learned societies and other communities are examples for investigation—not JORN partners or integrations. Route 1: recognised external review ¶ JORN checks reviewer qualifications and selection, conflicts, exact manuscript version, review criteria and scope, author response, revision history, transfer permission, transparency and procedural integrity. It may recognise the review in full, recognise only specified parts, request supplementary review, or decline recognition. Route 2: coordinated review ¶ Under a documented arrangement, a specialist community may recruit field experts while JORN coordinates open-science and technical assessment. Reports, data handling, conflicts and responsibilities remain clearly attributed. JORN retains the final editorial decision. Route 3: JORN internal review ¶ When no suitable community is available, the handling editor defines needed subject, methods, statistical, governance and technical expertise before recruiting reviewers. A medical-AI package, for example, may require a clinician, machine-learning or statistical-methods reviewer, privacy or data-governance specialist, and separate software evaluator. Recognition is evidence-specific ¶ Recognition applies to an exact review, version and scope—not automatically to every output of an organisation. It expires or requires reassessment when criteria, ownership, process, data handling or conflicts materially change. Relationships must be named correctly ¶ A recommended service is not a recognised assessment provider; a recognised provider is not necessarily integrated; and integration is not a formal partnership. All confirmed relationships must appear in the public relationship register . Final responsibility ¶ An unconflicted JORN editor decides whether review is sufficient, appoints supplementary expertise, assesses revisions and makes the accept or reject decision. External review does not guarantee JORN acceptance. Suggest a review community Read recognition criteria

docs.text: Status: network being designed. Joining an interest list will not itself make anyone a JORN reviewer or confer editorial authority. Appointment begins with an expertise profile ¶ Before recruitment, the handling editor records the subject knowledge, methods, statistics, community context, data governance and technical expertise needed. Selection should match that profile rather than institutional prestige, seniority alone or public volunteering. Recruitment routes ¶ JORN may use direct invitations, carefully moderated open calls, referrals from qualified communities, and the reviewer-interest register. An editor verifies identity, relevant work, current competence, availability and conflicts before formal appointment. Public comments may inform review but do not become commissioned peer review automatically. Safeguards ¶ Reviewers disclose financial, professional, personal, supervisory, competitive and advocacy conflicts. The editor documents eligibility and recusal. Confidential materials stay within approved systems. Harassment, coercion, discriminatory review and attempts to identify confidential participants are subject to moderation and complaint procedures. Identity and publication ¶ The proposed default is to publish reports, author responses and editorial decisions after acceptance. Reviewers may sign by informed consent or remain confidential. Any disclosure exception and reason should be recorded. Rejected or withdrawn reviews require separate policy and consent. Credit, workload and compensation ¶ JORN should offer citable reports where appropriate, ORCID-compatible credit, contribution acknowledgement and clear deadlines. Workload must be monitored; repeated unpaid labour must not be assumed. Honoraria or other compensation may be appropriate for intensive scientific, software, privacy, clinical or secure-data evaluation. Rates, eligibility, funding and tax treatment are [TO BE CONFIRMED] and cannot affect editorial outcomes. Join or nominate ¶ The reviewer-interest form, privacy notice, appointment criteria, conduct agreement and contact route are [TO BE CONFIRMED BEFORE COLLECTION OF PERSONAL DATA] . Explore all participation roles Read the scientific-review model

docs.text: Status: proposed model. Review communities, editors and reviewer appointments have not yet been confirmed. Disciplinary scientific review ¶ Qualified field reviewers assess the research question, relevance, scholarly contribution, methodology, statistical reasoning, interpretation, relationship to existing knowledge, supported conclusions, and relevant ethical or disciplinary standards. Open-science implementation review ¶ Reviewers assess repositories, identifiers, versions, licences, availability and restrictions, documentation, provenance, object connections and implementation levels. They do not convert openness into a claim of validity. Artifact and reproducibility assessment ¶ Technical evaluators make bounded findings about software function, data usability, environments, workflow execution, reproduced outputs, synthetic-data utility, or secure real-data verification. A successful execution is not independent replication or a publication decision. Review routes ¶ Recognised external community: JORN evaluates an existing qualified, version-specific review and may recognise all, part, or none of it. Coordinated external-community review: a field community recruits or coordinates scientific experts while JORN coordinates complementary assessments and retains the final decision. JORN internal review: when no suitable external community is available, a handling editor defines the expertise profile and appoints qualified reviewers. External review reduces duplication but does not transfer JORN’s responsibility. An unconflicted JORN editor evaluates sufficiency, fills expertise gaps, oversees revision, and accepts or rejects the exact version. Feedback is not automatically peer review ¶ Open community feedback is voluntary and not commissioned by an editor. JORN-commissioned peer review is performed by a qualified reviewer formally appointed by a JORN editor. Recognised external peer review is a formal external process accepted under published criteria. Public visibility alone does not change one category into another. Compare the review routes Learn about JORN reviewer recruitment

docs.text: Proposed founding scope. Formal submissions are not open, and the editorial board must approve detailed eligibility criteria before the pilot begins. Journal aim ¶ JORN aims to publish citable, accountable journal records for research that is distributed across manuscripts, data, software, environments, protocols, and repositories. Formal editorial assessment and independent peer review will determine whether work is accepted under the JORN title. The journal is designed to make the exact evidence and assessment scope traceable. Open availability is valuable, but openness alone does not establish validity or trust. Founding pilot scope ¶ The pilot will focus narrowly on independent publication and certification of reproducible computational research . JORN should accept only work for which it can appoint qualified editors, reviewers, and—where applicable—artifact or computational evaluators. Initial article types ¶ 01 Reproducible computational research articles Research in which code, data, environments, and computational workflows are central to the reported findings. 02 Research software and computational methods papers Software or methods with a clear research contribution, documentation, tests, an archived release, and an identifiable reuse case. 03 Reproducibility and replication reports Independent attempts to reproduce computational results or replicate prior findings, including informative unsuccessful attempts. Acceptance will not depend on receiving an optional evidence badge. Scientific peer review, artifact evaluation, and computational reproduction checks are related but distinct assessments. Common eligibility requirements ¶ Every submission should provide: a clear research contribution or well-defined reproduction or replication question; methods and computational reasoning reported in sufficient detail for review; an exact, persistently identified manuscript version; an archived software release, source repository, tests, and environment information where software is relevant; exact data identifiers and versions, or a documented restriction and reviewable access route; relevant protocols, preregistrations, funding, ethics, and competing-interest declarations; authorship, ORCID identifiers, and CRediT contribution roles; and licenses or access terms appropriate to each research object. Responsible restrictions ¶ Public access is not always ethical or lawful. Controlled access, an embargo, synthetic data, or non-sharing may be appropriate for privacy, consent, security, Indigenous or community governance, endangered species, confidential interviews, or protected health information. JORN will evaluate whether the restriction is justified and whether responsible review is possible. It will report the access category neutrally rather than treat restricted data as a lower score. Possible future scope ¶ Not accepted during the founding pilot: general dataset papers, non-computational methods papers, research-infrastructure reports, registered reports, broad multidisciplinary research articles, community-led research across all methodologies, and qualitative research requiring a distinct evaluation framework. These areas may be considered only after the journal has appropriate editorial expertise, policies, governance, evaluation criteria, and sustainable capacity. Listing them here is not a commitment to accept them later. Out of scope ¶ The pilot should decline work that falls outside the three article types; lacks a traceable manuscript, software release, environment, or evidence route; cannot be reviewed responsibly; has unresolved ethics or integrity concerns; or requires expertise and security JORN cannot provide. JORN is not a repository, preprint server, badge vendor, software-testing service, or venue for technical certification without formal journal review and an editorial decision. Long-term mission and controlled expansion ¶ JORN’s long-term mission is to support connected and certified open research records across disciplines. Expansion beyond the founding computational scope requires demonstrated editorial expertise, discipline-specific implementation guidance, appropriate review communities or internal reviewers, ethics and governance capacity, sustainable funding, and approved policies. Long-term ambition must not be presented as a current service.
